Profusa ( (PFSA) ) has provided an announcement.

On April 2, 2026, Profusa amended its existing securities purchase and pledge agreements, enabling the company to issue up to $12.2 million in additional senior secured convertible notes at a discount and reprioritizing repayment to favor these new instruments. As part of this financing, the company issued Ascent Partners Fund a $555,555.55 third-tranche note maturing in 2027, along with a warrant for 1,111,111 common shares at $0.50, reinforcing Profusa’s reliance on structured private capital to fund operations.

Separately, Profusa signed a non-binding letter of intent on March 31, 2026, revised April 3–4, 2026, to acquire Bio Insights’ PanOmics next-generation sequencing assets for $30 million in equity, plus a 3% revenue royalty. The transaction terms envisage issuing common and non-voting preferred stock convertible into tens of millions of shares over time, a seven-year lock-up for most of Bio Insights’ equity, and additional equity financing of $10 million to fund PanOmics validation and equipment. Governance and control features include a Bio Insights board nominee, a voting agreement in favor of Profusa board proposals, and new equity awards equal to 12% of fully diluted shares for Profusa’s CEO and CFO, aligning management retention with the success of the expanded oncology diagnostics platform.

More about Profusa

Profusa, Inc. is a Delaware-based medical technology company focused on real-time biochemical monitoring and genomic diagnostics, with an emerging strategy to build an integrated oncology platform. data.title: Penguin Solutions Pivots Hard Toward AI Memory Growth
data.description:
Penguin Solutions, Inc. ((PENG)) has held its Q2 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Penguin Solutions’ latest earnings call struck a cautiously upbeat tone, as management framed the quarter as a strategic pivot toward AI inference and memory solutions rather than a pure growth story. Robust momentum in the Integrated Memory business, upgraded full‑year guidance and a rich slate of AI‑focused product launches offset concerns about falling Advanced Computing revenue, rising inventories and looming margin pressure.

Raised Guidance Signals Confidence Despite Volatility

Penguin raised its full‑year midpoint outlook to 12% net sales growth and $2.15 in non‑GAAP diluted EPS, up from 6% growth and $2.00 previously. Management tied the upgrade to better‑than‑expected performance in memory, stronger pricing and growing visibility into AI‑related demand, even as other parts of the business retrench.

Integrated Memory Becomes Growth Engine

Integrated Memory delivered net sales of $172 million, accounting for half of company revenue and surging 63% year‑over‑year. The company now expects this segment to grow a powerful 65%–75% for the full year, citing intense AI‑driven demand and favorable memory pricing as key tailwinds for the new core franchise.

AI‑Focused Product Innovation Accelerates

Penguin introduced its MemoryAI product line, including CXL‑based MemoryAI servers and a MemoryAI KV Cache tailored for large language model inference. The firm is also advancing its Photonic Memory Appliance and expanding its OriginAI factory architectures, aiming to capture the next wave of inference‑centric AI deployments across data centers.

New AI Customers and Bookings Build Visibility

Bookings in non‑hyperscale AI and high‑performance computing were strong in the quarter, with five new AI/HPC customer logos, bringing the first‑half total to seven versus three a year ago. Wins included an enterprise voice AI deployment with Deepgram and Dell, an AI makerspace at Georgia Tech and a Tier 1 financial buyer for CXL‑powered KV Cache servers.

Margins Improve on Pricing and Mix

Non‑GAAP gross margin reached 31.2% in Q2, improving 0.4 percentage points from a year earlier and 1.2 points sequentially. Management credited the lift to better memory pricing, a favorable product mix and tariff recovery in the LED business, even as it warned that some of these benefits will fade in the second half.

Profits and Cash Flow Remain Resilient

Penguin posted non‑GAAP diluted EPS of $0.52, flat year‑over‑year, with adjusted EBITDA of $50 million, down 6% but up 11% versus the prior quarter. Operating income was $45 million, operating cash flow reached $55 million and the company ended the quarter in a net cash position, holding $489 million in cash and short‑term investments.

Partnerships and Leadership Bolster AI Strategy

The company emphasized ongoing strategic partnerships with NVIDIA, Dell and other ecosystem players to accelerate AI infrastructure offerings. It also appointed Ian Colle as senior vice president and chief product officer, a move aimed at sharpening product strategy and speeding execution across its growing AI roadmap.

Active Balance Sheet and Capital Returns

Management leaned on the balance sheet to secure critical inventory and strategic memory purchases, accepting higher working capital to support growth. At the same time, Penguin monetized its stake in Celestial AI for about $32 million, repurchased roughly $32 million of stock, or 1.7 million shares, and reduced debt to $450 million with no major maturities before 2029.

Headline Revenue Declines Mask Business Mix Shift

Total net sales fell 6% year‑over‑year to $343 million, reflecting deployment timing and a deliberate shift away from certain legacy and hyperscale‑heavy exposures. While the headline decline may unsettle some investors, management argued that the underlying mix is rotating toward higher‑potential AI and memory franchises.

Advanced Computing Hit by Business Wind‑Down

Advanced Computing net sales dropped to $116 million, down 42% from a year earlier and now just a third of company revenue. For the year, management expects this segment to decline 25%–15% as it winds down the Penguin Edge business and books no hyperscaler AI hardware sales, a combination that sharply crimps growth in the near term.

Product Sales Under Pressure During Transition

Product net sales of $279 million were down 8% year‑over‑year, as large projects proved lumpy and the company continued to reduce its reliance on hyperscale customers. Penguin framed this as a transition period, arguing that near‑term product weakness is the price of repositioning toward more diversified and AI‑driven revenue streams.

LED Segment Softens with Lower Tariff Tailwinds

Optimized LED net sales came in at $56 million, down 7% and representing 16% of company revenue. For the full year, management now expects LED sales to decline 15%–5%, and it sees reduced tariff recovery in the second half, which could squeeze margins even as the segment remains strategically less central than AI and memory.

Inventory Build Raises Working Capital Needs

Inventory climbed to $322 million from $200 million a year earlier, lifting days inventory to 51 from 37 as Penguin bought strategically and absorbed higher memory input costs. The build drove up working capital and pushed accounts payable to $401 million, underscoring the capital intensity of preparing for sustained AI‑driven demand.

Gross Margin Set to Compress in Second Half

Despite better recent margins, Penguin cut its full‑year non‑GAAP gross margin outlook to about 28%, a one‑point reduction. The company expects a greater mix of lower‑margin memory, higher memory component costs in AI hardware and reduced LED tariff recovery to weigh on profitability as the year progresses.

Supply‑Chain and Timing Risks Cloud Revenue Conversion

Management highlighted extended lead times and constrained availability, especially in memory, which stretch deployment cycles and push revenue recognition out by roughly three to six months. These delays create quarter‑to‑quarter volatility and limit the firm’s ability to quickly translate robust bookings into reported net sales.

Wind‑Down of Penguin Edge Amplifies Headwinds

The orderly exit from the high‑margin Penguin Edge business, combined with the lack of hyperscaler AI hardware orders, is expected to trim about 14 percentage points from total net sales growth this year. Within Advanced Computing specifically, the impact is even harsher, shaving roughly 30 percentage points off what segment growth would otherwise have been.

Guidance Underscores AI‑Led Growth and Margin Trade‑Offs

Looking ahead, Penguin’s raised FY‑26 outlook calls for 12% net sales growth and $2.15 in non‑GAAP EPS, with gross margin around 28% and operating expenses near $250 million. Segment guidance highlights a stark contrast: Advanced Computing down 25%–15%, LED down 15%–5%, and Integrated Memory up a powerful 65%–75%, all under assumptions of no hyperscaler hardware sales and a full Penguin Edge wind‑down.

Penguin Solutions’ earnings call painted a company in active transition, trading near‑term revenue and margin softness for a deeper stake in AI inference and memory. Execution on supply, inventory and bookings conversion will be crucial, but if management delivers, the emerging memory‑centric portfolio could reshape the company’s earnings power over the next few years.

data.title: Sidus Space Earnings Call Balances Progress And Pain
data.description:
Sidus Space, Inc. Class A ((SIDU)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Sidus Space’s latest earnings call painted a mixed picture, pairing notable technical and operational strides with worsening near-term financial metrics. Management stressed that the company is deliberately trading short-term profitability and legacy revenue for scalable, higher-margin product and platform businesses, leaving investors weighing impressive execution in orbit against sizable losses and dilution on the ground.

Operational Milestones: Three Satellites Launched and Performing

Sidus highlighted the launch of three LizzieSat satellites between March 2024 and March 2025 as a core proof point of its space platform strategy. LizzieSat-3 has completed full commissioning with pointing accuracy better than 30 arc seconds and is already supporting recurring customer payloads, while LizzieSat-1 has finished its mission and LizzieSat-2 continues commissioning in a more challenging equatorial orbit.

Onboard Compute and Edge AI: Fortis VPX Moves Toward Revenue

The company showcased progress in commercializing onboard compute and edge AI through its Fortis VPX modular computing system. Fortis, which includes a rugged single-board computer and PNT card for GPS-denied environments, is beginning customer deployment and is being evaluated for satellite payload processing, unmanned platforms and ground-based operations.

Defense Access: MDA SHIELD Opens National Security Pathways

Sidus scored a strategic win by gaining access to the Missile Defense Agency’s SHIELD IDIQ contract vehicle, a 10-year procurement framework. This positions the company to compete for missile defense, persistent sensing and real-time data processing programs, aligning its space and compute offerings with large-scale U.S. national security opportunities.

Lunar, GEO and Partnerships: Expanding the Mission Pipeline

Management detailed a growing pipeline beyond low Earth orbit, including an integration deal for Lonestar’s Commercial Pathfinder mission on LizzieSat-5, for which a milestone payment has been received. The company also introduced its LunarLizzie concept, signed an MOU to support a GEO platform and partnered with Simera Sense to push AI-enabled hyperspectral imaging capabilities.

Liquidity Strength: Cash Up Sharply and Debt Cleared

From a balance-sheet standpoint, Sidus ended FY2025 with $43.2 million in cash, up roughly 175% from $15.7 million a year earlier, supported by multiple equity raises. The company entered 2026 with no outstanding term debt, giving it financial runway to execute its product and platform roadmap despite ongoing operating losses.

Vertically Integrated Model: Retaining IP Across the Stack

Sidus emphasized its fully integrated approach, developing hardware, software and data capabilities in-house and retaining the associated intellectual property. A 24/7 mission control center, now in its third year, manages both the company’s own constellation and third-party customers, supporting its ambition to deliver end-to-end space-based solutions.

Software-Defined Satellites: Upgrades Without New Launches

The company underscored the benefits of its software-defined satellite architecture, which has already enabled on-orbit upgrades like enhanced autonomous navigation and FatherEdge100i. This approach allows Sidus to roll out new capabilities without fresh hardware or launches, potentially extending satellite lifetimes and lowering long-term upgrade and replacement costs.

Cost Discipline and Systems: Building for Scale

To prepare for scaled operations, Sidus implemented a new ERP system and executed targeted cost-reduction initiatives. The sales organization has been refocused on higher-value commercial and defense opportunities, with management arguing that this will support recurring revenue growth and margin expansion once current investments begin to convert into larger contracts.

Revenue Decline: Strategic Transition Hits the Top Line

For FY2025, revenue fell to $3.4 million from $4.7 million in FY2024, a 28% decline that management framed as an intentional consequence of exiting legacy contract manufacturing. Timing of milestones also weighed on reported results, and leadership cautioned that the shift toward product and platform sales may continue to pressure revenue in the near term.

Rising Costs and Widening Gross Losses

Cost of revenue climbed 48% year over year to $9.1 million, reflecting heavier investment in satellites and supporting infrastructure. As a result, reported gross loss widened to $5.7 million from $1.5 million, and even when excluding depreciation, the company swung from a modest gross profit in FY2024 to a gross loss of $1.7 million in FY2025.

Higher SG&A and a Larger Net Loss Burden

Selling, general and administrative expenses surged to $22.3 million from $14.2 million, driven by additional headcount, benefits, equity-based compensation, mission operations spending and a $4.5 million impairment tied to LizzieSat-1. The heavier cost structure, combined with ramping depreciation, pushed the net loss to $29.5 million compared with $17.5 million a year earlier.

Adjusted EBITDA, Impairment and Depreciation Headwinds

On a non-GAAP basis, adjusted EBITDA loss deteriorated to $17.3 million from $12.9 million, underscoring that cash operating performance has yet to catch up with the company’s growth investments. A $4.5 million impairment related to LS-1 assets and an additional $2.1 million of depreciation on satellite and software investments added significant noncash pressure to the bottom line.

Operational Risk: Equatorial Orbit Slows LizzieSat-2

The company acknowledged operational timing risk tied to LizzieSat-2, which was placed in an equatorial inclination to deliberately test different environments. This orbit leads to fewer ground-station passes and lengthens commissioning, delaying the point at which the satellite can fully support commercial missions and generate recurring revenue.

Dilution and Volatile Shares Weigh on Investor Sentiment

To fund its strategy, Sidus issued about 47.1 million Class A shares in 2025, raising roughly $53.3 million and materially diluting existing holders. Management recognized investor unease with stock volatility and the uncertain pace of commercialization, while arguing that strengthened liquidity was necessary to unlock the value of its space and defense platforms.

Strategic Transition Keeps Near-Term Revenue Under Pressure

The exit from legacy contract manufacturing in favor of dual-use products and platforms remains a central theme of Sidus’s strategy, but it comes with short-term pain. Because product and platform deals often feature milestone-based recognition and longer sales cycles, reported revenue could remain uneven until bookings and deployments reach greater scale.

Forward-Looking Focus: Commercialization, Defense Wins and Efficiency

Management refrained from offering numeric 2026 guidance but anchored expectations around accelerating commercialization and converting its growing defense and commercial pipeline into contracted programs. The company plans to prioritize higher-margin product and platform revenue, deploy Fortis VPX with customers, advance LizzieSat-4 and LizzieSat-5 production, maintain strict cost control and leverage its $43.2 million cash balance and debt-free status to bridge the next 12 to 18 months.

Sidus Space’s earnings call ultimately balanced ambition with caution, showcasing real technical progress and a fortressed balance sheet against a backdrop of shrinking revenue, larger losses and significant dilution. For investors, the story now hinges on whether the company can translate its on-orbit capabilities and defense positioning into repeatable, higher-margin revenue before the market’s patience runs thin.

data.description:
Bassett Furniture Industries, Incorporated ((BSET)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Bassett Furniture Industries, Inc. used its latest earnings call to strike a cautiously optimistic tone, acknowledging a tough first quarter while pointing to strong liquidity, accelerating e‑commerce, and targeted growth initiatives. Management framed weather disruptions, margin pressure, and negative cash flow as near‑term challenges, counterbalanced by a solid balance sheet and a clear plan to restore profitability.

Strong Liquidity and Ongoing Shareholder Returns

Bassett closed the quarter with $51.0 million in cash and short‑term investments, giving it meaningful financial flexibility despite negative operating cash flow. The company continued to prioritize shareholder returns, paying $1.7 million in dividends, approving a $0.20 per‑share payout for late May, and executing modest share repurchases of $147,000.

E‑commerce Acceleration Drives Order Growth

Digital performance was a bright spot as e‑commerce conversion surged 130% in the quarter, powering a 28% increase in online orders. Management highlighted several quarters of double‑digit e‑commerce growth and the extension of national home delivery into previously unserved areas, underscoring omnichannel as a key growth engine.

Custom Upholstery and New Products Show Strength

The company’s core true custom upholstery business, its largest category, posted a 6% rise in retail written sales during Q1, bucking the softer top‑line trend. Bassett also reported encouraging consumer response to refreshed case goods and new offerings such as the Z4 Sleeper and HideAway dining, signaling product relevance amid a choppy macro backdrop.

Lane Venture Momentum and Outdoor Realignment

Lane Venture continued to gain traction, with overall brand shipments climbing 32% when retail shipments are included and wholesale shipments alone rising 2.6%. Bassett’s decision to fold its outdoor operations into Lane Venture has shifted the mix toward domestically produced aluminum, which now makes up 45% of outdoor sales and strengthens customization capabilities.

Strategic Growth Plan with New Stores and Design Focus

Management laid out a five‑point growth roadmap focused on comparable‑store gains, new store openings, omnichannel expansion, Bassett Design Centers and Custom Studios, and deeper interior design and hospitality penetration. Concrete moves include planned openings in Cincinnati and Orlando and a showroom relocation, designed to better showcase the brand and capture higher‑value design‑driven business.

Operational Efficiencies and Cost‑Saving Measures

To defend margins, Bassett is reshaping its organization and technology platform and has identified $1.5–$2.0 million in expected annual savings starting late in the second quarter. These efforts aim to offset inflation and lower volume while preserving the capacity to invest in growth initiatives, which management sees as critical to a sustainable recovery.

Revenue Decline Amid Challenging Quarter

Consolidated revenue slipped to $80.3 million, a 2.2% year‑over‑year decline equal to a $1.8 million drop. Retail revenue fell by $0.7 million while external wholesale revenue decreased by $1.1 million, reflecting softer demand and disruptions that weighed on both channels.

Retail Margin Pressure from Tariffs

Consolidated gross margin narrowed to 56.2%, down 80 basis points, with retail margins under the most strain. Retail gross margin declined 170 basis points to 51.5% as Bassett chose to absorb higher tariffs through mid‑January before implementing price increases, a strategy that temporarily suppressed profitability to protect traffic and market share.

Profitability Under Strain

Operating income nearly halved to $1.2 million, representing 1.4% of sales versus 3.0% in the prior year period, signaling weaker operating leverage. Diluted earnings per share fell to $0.13 from $0.21, highlighting how modest revenue declines and compressed margins translated into disproportionately lower bottom‑line results.

Wholesale and Open Market Softness

Wholesale gross margins were also pressured, slipping roughly 50 basis points as pricing and cost dynamics tightened. Shipments to the open market declined 5.3%, only partly offset by growth in other channels, underscoring a tougher environment for wholesale partners and independent retailers.

Severe Weather Disruptions Hit Sales

Mid‑January storms dealt an additional blow, forcing the closure of more than half of Bassett’s retail fleet for one weekend and over a quarter of stores the following weekend. Management noted that these shutdowns disrupted both retail and wholesale distribution patterns, with some sales likely lost outright and others merely deferred.

Negative Cash Flow and Rising Capital Spending

Seasonally weak first‑quarter conditions produced operating cash flow of negative $5.5 million, putting added focus on cash discipline even as growth investments ramp up. Capital expenditures are projected to rise to $8–$12 million in 2026, up from $4.5 million, reflecting spending on new stores and strategic showroom moves to support future growth.

Higher SG&A Burden Weighs on Leverage

Selling, general, and administrative expenses, excluding new store preopening, climbed to 54.7% of sales, up 70 basis points as lower volume eroded expense leverage. Retail SG&A increased by 20 basis points, and management cautioned that preopening costs of $200,000–$400,000 per new store will add near‑term overhead even as they pursue longer‑term gains.

Tariff and Input Cost Uncertainty

Retail margins were further squeezed by the lag between tariff increases and Bassett’s own pricing actions, which did not fully take effect until mid‑January. Rising fuel and commodity costs for materials like foam and poly, along with potential freight surcharges, remain a concern and may necessitate additional price adjustments to protect profitability.

Guidance and Forward‑Looking Outlook

Looking ahead, management is leaning on cost control and targeted investment, expecting $1.5–$2.0 million of annual savings to begin late in Q2 and improved retail margins following recent pricing moves. With capex of $8–$12 million planned for 2026 to fund new stores and relocations, solid liquidity of $51 million, and growth levers in e‑commerce, custom upholstery, and Lane Venture, Bassett aims to navigate near‑term margin and cash‑flow pressures while positioning for recovery.

MSC Industrial Direct struck a cautiously upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, as management balanced solid margin gains and strong cash generation against softer volumes and macro headwinds. Steady Top Line Growth Despite Volume Weakness

Management said pricing contributed roughly 6.5% to growth, underscoring the company’s ability to pass through higher costs, but also noted that the volume shortfall meant ADS landed below the midpoint of prior expectations.

Margins March Higher as Pricing and Productivity Kick In

Gross margin improved to 41.1%, up 10 basis points from a year ago and about 40 basis points sequentially, helped by pricing and mix. Adjusted operating margin reached 7.5%, a 40 basis point improvement, with adjusted incremental margins of 21% signaling that cost discipline and efficiency efforts are beginning to leverage even modest revenue growth.

Earnings and EPS Outpace Revenue Gains

Profitability outgrew sales, with GAAP EPS rising to $0.76 from $0.70 a year earlier. Adjusted EPS climbed to $0.82 from $0.72, a 14% increase, reflecting a combination of higher margins, controlled expenses and the benefits of the company’s ongoing operational optimization initiatives.

Cash Flow Strengthens Balance Sheet Flexibility

The balance sheet remains solid with net debt of about $466 million, or roughly 1.2 times EBITDA, giving the company room to maneuver. Operating cash flow conversion reached 224% in the quarter and free cash flow conversion was about 173%, keeping the full-year goal of generating roughly 90% of net income as free cash flow firmly in sight.

Vending and In-Plant Solutions Drive Sticky Growth

The company’s high-touch solutions engine continued to gain traction, with installed vending machines up 8% year over year to about 30,400 units. In-Plant programs grew 9% to 423, and combined vending and In-Plant sales rose roughly 8%, with each accounting for about 20% of total net sales, reinforcing their role as key growth and share-gain drivers.

Q3 Outlook Signals Reacceleration in Sales and Margins

Management guided fiscal Q3 average daily sales growth to a stronger 5%–7%, with pricing still doing much of the heavy lifting. Adjusted operating margin is expected between 9.7% and 10.3%, implying about 25% incremental margins at the midpoint and underscoring confidence in moving toward mid-single-digit full-year growth and improved profitability.

Operational Productivity and Inventory Actions Bear Fruit

Adjusted operating expenses fell 20 basis points as a percentage of sales despite higher absolute spending, thanks to headcount optimization and network efficiencies. Productivity improvements in planning, procurement and distribution centers, supported by AI tools, translated into better inventory management and lower distribution center headcount and compensation trends.

Disciplined Capital Allocation and Shareholder Returns

MSC continued to return cash to shareholders, distributing about $49 million in Q2 and $110 million year to date through dividends and buybacks. Capital expenditures were roughly $21 million in the quarter, down about $9 million from a year ago, with full-year capex still targeted at $100 million to $110 million including cloud investments, reflecting measured spending.

Sales Force Overhaul Weighs on Volume

The company’s final phase of sales optimization, which affected roughly 130 customer-facing roles and contributed to a net reduction of more than 400 field heads over 12 months, created short-term friction. Management cited relationship changes, elevated attrition and temporary customer coverage gaps as key reasons volumes declined about 4% and ADS missed the prior outlook midpoint.

Weather and Government Dynamics Add to Top-Line Drag

Adverse weather and a partial federal government shutdown combined to shave around 100 basis points off growth, according to management. Public sector daily sales slipped roughly 1%, as delayed activity and project pushouts weighed on demand, adding another layer of transitory pressure on quarterly performance.

Input Cost Inflation Squeezes Cutting Tool Economics

The company is contending with sharp cost inflation in cutting tools, particularly tungsten and carbide, where scrap prices have surged and suppliers are pushing through 7%–15% increases. Management plans further price actions around late spring and flagged continued pressure across parts of the supplier base, even as it works to preserve margins without derailing customer demand.

Higher Operating Costs Partially Offset by Savings

On an absolute basis, adjusted operating expenses rose about $7 million year over year, driven mainly by roughly $9 million in higher personnel-related costs and increased depreciation from digital and web investments. Additional outbound freight and incremental investment spending were partially offset by productivity measures, helping keep expenses manageable relative to sales.

Working Capital Build Temporarily Uses Cash

Working capital was a use of cash in the quarter, largely due to a deliberate inventory build aimed at supporting service levels and mitigating supply risk. The company also amended its accounts receivable securitization facility to add $50 million of capacity, which affected quarter comparisons but did not change the underlying trend that working capital absorbed cash in Q2.

Watching Geopolitics and Fuel Costs as Risk Factors

Management pointed to heightened geopolitical tensions and rising fuel prices as external risk factors that could disrupt supply chains or pressure costs. While no significant disruptions were reported, the company is monitoring developments closely, underscoring that the broader macro environment remains uncertain despite currently steady operations.

Guidance Underscores Confidence in Margin and Cash Goals

Looking ahead, the company reaffirmed its full-year framework, including mid-single-digit sales growth, roughly 20% adjusted incremental margins and free cash flow around 90% of net income. data.description:
Hilton Food ((GB:HFG)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions

Hilton Food’s latest earnings call painted a cautiously balanced picture for investors. Management highlighted a resilient core meat and fresh prepared foods business, strong liquidity and value realized from disposals, yet acknowledged serious drag from seafood and plant-based units, heavy exceptional costs and a step down in profit guidance for 2026.

Core meat business holds its ground

Hilton’s core meat and fresh prepared foods operations, which generate about 90% of group revenue, proved notably resilient. Volumes from continuing operations inched up 0.2%, with U.K. meat volumes only slightly lower despite beef prices surging more than 30%, while APAC posted volume growth and Central Europe saw double-digit gains in fresh prepared foods.

Revenue up, driven more by inflation than demand

On a constant-currency basis, revenue rose 11.9% year-on-year, but this was largely the result of higher raw material input costs rather than strong underlying demand. Volume trends were broadly flat, underscoring that Hilton is passing through inflation rather than enjoying a genuine volume-led top-line acceleration.

Profits edge lower but dividends progress

Adjusted profit before tax came in at GBP 73.2m, slipping around 2.8–3% from the prior year. From continuing operations, excluding Fairfax Meadow, PBT was GBP 69.0m, down about 1%, yet the company still maintained its progressive dividend stance, lifting the full-year dividend per share 1.4% to 35p while holding the final dividend flat.

Balance sheet strength and fresh liquidity

Net bank debt nudged down to GBP 126.7m, leaving leverage at a comfortable 0.9x net debt-to-EBITDA. Hilton also secured a new GBP 450m revolving credit facility with at least a five-year term, giving the group ample funding flexibility to support ongoing investment and manage near-term volatility.

Disposals unlock value and cash for shareholders

The company realized profits on disposals of GBP 66.5m from the sales of Fairfax Meadow and Foods Connected, simplifying its portfolio and crystallizing value. Hilton returned GBP 31.5m to shareholders during 2025, underlining a willingness to recycle capital from non-core assets while rewarding investors.

International growth projects move ahead

Expansion in Canada and Saudi Arabia is progressing, with around GBP 55m invested so far in the Canadian project and production testing scheduled for the second half. Management expects Canada to start contributing commercially from 2027, reaching full contribution by 2029, while a planned investment of up to GBP 30m in Poland aims at fresh prepared foods with a targeted ROCE above 20%.

Strategy reset with clear financial targets

Hilton laid out a refreshed strategy focused on maximizing the core, enhancing the product mix and expanding geographically into attractive markets. The medium-term framework calls for mid-single-digit average operating profit growth, around 100% cash conversion on average and a group return on capital employed of at least 20% over time.

Seafood and plant-based units drag performance

Seafood plus vegetarian and vegan volumes dropped 2.6%, with Seachill sliding into a loss-making position and these units collectively weighing heavily on group profit. Management identified these businesses, including Seachill, Foppen and Dalco, as the principal reasons for the expected profit drop in 2026 and a core focus for turnaround efforts.

Heavy exceptional charges and write-offs

Hilton booked inventory write-offs of GBP 18.4m, alongside Foppen-related relocation and airfreight cash costs of GBP 9.2m and reorganization and restructuring costs of GBP 9.6m, more than double the prior year. Overall, exceptional and transformation-related cash flows reached GBP 18.9m, significantly distorting reported profitability versus underlying trends.

Margins under pressure as operating profit falls

Constant-currency operating profit declined 4.4% and the operating margin narrowed to 2.3% from 2.6% a year earlier, signaling profitability pressure despite revenue growth. Adjusted EPS was 56p, down 7.4%, reflecting lower profits and a slightly higher effective tax rate, which together reduced earnings power for shareholders.

Operational setbacks in challenged segments persist

Foppen continues to face regulatory constraints in Greece that require relocating production, driving higher operating and exceptional costs and operational disruption. Dalco remains loss-making despite some improvement, while salmon prices around 17% lower in 2025 further squeezed Foppen’s revenues and contributed to ongoing segment underperformance.

Free cash outflow and higher near-term CapEx

The group generated a free cash outflow in 2025 even after disposal proceeds and expects net debt to rise in 2026 as investment ramps up. Core CapEx is guided at GBP 50m–55m in 2026 and overall spend will be elevated as the Canada project completes and the Poland expansion starts, adding to short-term cash and leverage pressure.

Persistent market and supply-side risks

Hilton highlighted significant raw material inflation, including more than 30% beef inflation in the U.K., as a structural challenge. Freight cost uncertainty, worsened by geopolitical developments in the Middle East, and the risk that cost-of-living pressures weaken consumer demand all threaten volumes and margins if not offset by pricing and efficiency gains.

Guidance signals a profit step-down before recovery

Management reaffirmed 2026 adjusted PBT guidance of GBP 60m–65m, implying a mid-teens percentage decline from the GBP 73.2m delivered in 2025, mainly due to continued issues in seafood and plant-based units. While ROCE stood at 20.1% in 2025 and may dip below 20% in 2026 amid elevated CapEx and restructuring spend, the company is targeting around 100% cash-flow conversion, disciplined investment and mid-single-digit operating profit growth over the medium term.

Hilton Food’s earnings call ultimately framed 2026 as a reset year, with strong core operations and a solid balance sheet offset by problematic non-core units and higher investment needs. For investors, the story hinges on whether management can execute its turnaround in seafood and plant-based, deliver on its new projects and restore profit growth and returns beyond the near-term earnings dip.

Lovesac’s latest earnings call painted a mixed picture for investors. Management highlighted modest revenue growth, healthy cash generation and strategic gains in digital, showrooms and product innovation, yet near‑term margins remain under pressure from tariffs, freight and promotions, leaving the outlook cautious despite long‑term confidence.

Revenue Growth and Balance Sheet Resilience

Lovesac closed the year with net sales of $697.1 million, up 2.4% year over year, and Q4 revenue of $248 million, up 2.7%. The company generated positive free cash flow, ended Q4 with $101.9 million in cash, no debt and cut inventory by 14%, giving it solid financial flexibility heading into a tougher FY27.

Digital Momentum Driving Internet Sales

Internet sales surged 12.3% in Q4 to $79.2 million as web demand grew double digits and Cyber Monday more than doubled versus last year. Record web customer satisfaction and strong online conversion suggest recent digital leadership changes and a more sophisticated e‑commerce strategy are starting to pay off.

Showroom Expansion and Rising Productivity

Showroom net sales rose 3.5% in Q4 to $159.8 million, driven by 21 net new locations as the fleet grew to about 278 stores. The latest showroom cohort is tracking to one‑year cash paybacks, while enhanced field incentives lifted associate productivity by the mid‑teens percent, supporting the omnichannel thesis.

Product Innovation Anchored by Reclining Seat

The Reclining Seat exceeded expectations, appearing in roughly one of every three new Sactional setups in FY26 and driving repeat purchases, with existing customers representing about 40% of Reclining Seat sales. Other product lines, including Snugg, jumped 191.9% in Q4 and Sactionals grew 1%, underscoring the strength of the modular platform.

Onshoring and Supply Chain Repositioning

Lovesac exited FY26 with no production in China, down from about 50% a few years ago, and is now accelerating domestic manufacturing. The company plans to start U.S. production of Sactionals seat inserts this summer, expecting onshoring to be gross‑margin neutral over time while reducing cost volatility and shortening lead times.

Capital Allocation and Buyback Firepower

The board approved an additional $40 million share repurchase authorization, bringing total capacity to roughly $54.1 million. Management signaled it will be opportunistic in buying back stock while still funding growth, including about $20 million in capex and around eight net new showrooms in the coming year.

Marketing Evolution and Brand Engagement

The company completed its brand evolution and adopted a digital‑first marketing strategy, leaning into performance channels during the holidays. Black Friday and Cyber Monday campaigns delivered strong engagement and conversion, and management pointed to early improvements in return on ad spend as proof the new playbook is working.

Resale and Circularity as Growth Channels

The “Loved by Lovesac” resale platform launched in 29 states and is emerging as a powerful acquisition tool. Around 70% of resale buyers were new customers, suggesting that circularity can both strengthen the brand and create a profitable lifetime‑value engine as the program scales.

Margin Compression from Tariffs and Freight

Gross margin in Q4 declined 230 basis points to 58.1% from 60.4% a year earlier, with inbound transportation and tariff increases alone adding about 300 bps of pressure and outbound logistics another 90 bps. Higher promotional discounting further weighed on profitability, partially offsetting product‑level margin improvements.

Earnings and EBITDA Under Pressure

Full‑year net income slipped to $4.1 million, while Q4 net income fell to $32.1 million from $35.3 million in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 declined to $49.6 million from $53.9 million, reflecting the combined impact of gross margin compression and higher operating costs.

Profitability Headwinds and Conservative Q1 Outlook

Management guided to an adjusted EBITDA loss of $12 million to $16 million in Q1 FY27, with an estimated net loss of $14 million to $18 million and a diluted loss per share between $0.95 and $1.22. The company expects the home furnishings category to remain soft and is planning FY27 cautiously, accepting near‑term earnings pressure while strategic initiatives ramp.

Product and Channel Weak Spots

Not all segments are firing: Q4 Sacs sales dropped 18.2%, and “Other” net sales fell 45.4% to $9 million, largely due to the end of the Best Buy shop‑in‑shop partnership. Management also noted that transactions below $6,000 remain under pressure, pointing to continued consumer caution on smaller ticket purchases.

Macro, Tariffs and Category Headwinds

The company flagged ongoing geopolitical and tariff uncertainty, including shifting import regimes and volatile shipping costs, as key external risks. The broader category declined around 3.3% in Q4 and 3.4% for the year, and Lovesac is assuming continued low‑single‑digit industry contraction in its FY27 plans.

Tax Rate and Operating Expense Dynamics

Lovesac’s effective tax rate reached 39% for the year, including 29.6% in Q4, influenced by a deferred tax asset shortfall tied to prior equity compensation and low pretax income. SG&A rose in dollar terms, with Q4 costs up $2.2 million, driven largely by $7.9 million in higher payroll and incentive expenses as the company invests in people.

Multi‑Year Path Back to Higher Margins

Management cautioned that meaningful gross margin recovery will be a multi‑year process, as onshoring benefits require time to flow through inventory and sales. With current tariffs and shipping costs still elevated, Lovesac expects near‑term margins to remain pressured, with more substantial relief likely beyond FY27.

Guidance and Outlook for Fiscal 2027

For FY27, Lovesac projects net sales of $700 million to $750 million, adjusted EBITDA of $33 million to $44 million and gross margin of 56% to 57%, with net income between $5 million and $14 million and EPS of $0.34 to $0.95. The outlook assumes roughly eight net new showrooms, about $20 million in capex, advertising near 12% of sales, SG&A at 40% to 41% and a category that shrinks slightly while tariffs and shipping remain a drag.

Lovesac’s earnings call reveals a company balancing resilience with realism, leveraging a strong balance sheet, digital momentum and manufacturing shifts to offset industry and macro pressure. While near‑term profitability will be constrained, management’s multi‑year strategy around innovation, omnichannel growth and onshoring positions the brand to emerge stronger when conditions normalize.

Scaling Managed Hashrate and Power Footprint

By the end of 2025, the company managed 26.1 exahash per second of computing power, including 3.7 EH/s self‑owned and 22.4 EH/s from third parties. Total available power capacity reached 478 megawatts, with 164 MW under its control in Ethiopia and the U.S., and management noted diversification of hashrate suppliers to reduce concentration risk.

Positive Adjusted EBITDA and Growing Bitcoin Holdings

Bitfufu posted positive adjusted EBITDA of $8.3 million for 2025, signaling that the core business can generate operating cash flow even in a tough mining environment. The company’s Bitcoin treasury grew from 1,720 BTC to 1,778 BTC by year‑end, and further to 1,830 BTC by late February, while pledged coins fell sharply, improving financial flexibility.

Equipment Sales and Hosting Services Accelerate

Beyond cloud mining, equipment and services emerged as meaningful growth levers, with mining hardware sales soaring 76.4% to $53.7 million. Hosting and other revenue nearly doubled to $8.4 million, buoyed by the miner‑with‑hosting program launched in the second half of 2025, which management views as a scalable complement to its core offerings.

Liquidity Bolstered by Undrawn Credit Facility

Cash and cash equivalents stood at $27.8 million at the end of 2025, a relatively modest cushion given the volatility of Bitcoin mining economics. However, the company pointed to $85 million of remaining capacity on a $100 million revolving credit facility as an important liquidity backstop to fund working capital and selective strategic moves.

Strategic Focus on Discipline and Hybrid Model

Executives reiterated a commitment to disciplined capital allocation, avoiding aggressive leverage despite access to credit. Bitfufu continues to emphasize its hybrid model combining cloud services and self‑mining, supported by its proprietary Aladdin platform, and aims to turn its controlled power capacity into a sustainable cost advantage.

Net Loss Driven by Impairments and Fair Value Swings

Despite operational gains, the bottom line deteriorated sharply, with a net loss of $57.4 million replacing last year’s $54.0 million profit. Management attributed the reversal mainly to non‑cash impairment charges on mining equipment and larger unrealized fair value losses on Bitcoin‑linked receivables and payables, along with lower realized gains on asset sales.

Self‑Mining Revenue Plunges Amid Tough Economics

Self‑mining revenue collapsed to $63.1 million from $157.5 million, shrinking to just 13% of total sales as higher network difficulty and weaker hash price squeezed profitability. The steep decline underscores why the company is leaning more heavily into cloud services and customer‑driven hashrate rather than pure proprietary mining.

Margin Pressure and Elevated Mining Cash Costs

Gross profit after depreciation and amortization came in at $26.7 million, with gross margin slipping to 5.7% from 6.4%, reflecting a tougher operating backdrop. The blended cash cost to mine one Bitcoin averaged $77,573, with owned machines at roughly $61,000 per coin and leased capacity around $98,000, highlighting the drag from higher‑cost leased hashrate.

Weaker Cash Position and Balance Sheet Sensitivities

The company’s cash balance declined to $27.8 million from $45.1 million as it navigated a challenging year for mining profitability. Bitfufu also carried $24.1 million in digital asset collateral receivables tied to pledged Bitcoin, signaling that while reliance on secured borrowing is easing, its balance sheet remains sensitive to digital asset price swings.

Customer Revenue Efficiency Moderates

Net dollar retention dropped to 100% from 117%, meaning Bitfufu essentially held revenue flat from existing customers on a year‑over‑year basis instead of expanding it as it did previously. Management framed this as a normalization after a strong 2024, but investors may watch closely to see whether upselling and cross‑selling can re‑accelerate.

Guidance Points to Cloud Growth and Cost Optimization

Looking ahead to 2026, management plans to double down on cloud mining growth, customer retention, and expansion of managed hashrate and power capacity, including potential moves in North America and the Middle East. The strategy prioritizes fleet upgrades to lower the cash cost to mine, a reduced reliance on leased capacity, preservation of liquidity, and disciplined, return‑driven capacity additions while gradually building the Bitcoin treasury.

Bitfufu’s earnings call presents a tale of two tracks: strong strategic progress in cloud mining, services, and Bitcoin accumulation, contrasted with pressured margins, a swing to loss, and higher‑than‑ideal mining costs. For investors, the key question is whether the company’s disciplined expansion and cost‑reduction plans can translate its operational momentum into more resilient and sustainable earnings over the next cycle.

Operating companies logged more than $50 million in new contracted production bookings in the first quarter of 2026, a sharp step‑up that management cast as the strongest signal yet of pending revenue scale. These orders, largely from volume contracts, are not fully reflected in current revenue but are expected to convert into meaningful top‑line growth and enterprise value over the coming quarters.

Accelsius: Institutional Validation at $665 Million Valuation

Accelsius secured a second tranche of its $65 million Series B in December 2025, led by strategic investors Johnson Controls and Legrand at a roughly $665 million post‑money valuation. Management highlighted this as powerful third‑party validation of both the two‑phase direct‑to‑chip cooling technology and its commercial readiness for the data‑center market.

Accelsius Revenue Surge and Path to Cash Breakeven

Accelsius revenue jumped from $0.3 million in 2024 to $1.6 million in 2025, an increase of about 433 percent off a small base. The company expects Accelsius to reach cash‑flow breakeven by December 2026, implying an annualized revenue run‑rate near $100 million if it can execute against its contracted backlog and navigate supply‑chain constraints.

Consolidated Revenue Momentum from Early Commercialization

Across the platform, Innventure’s consolidated revenue climbed to $2.1 million in 2025 from $1.2 million in 2024, a roughly 75 percent rise. Management emphasized that this modest top‑line still reflects only the early stages of commercialization, with the bulk of contracted revenue expected to show up as hardware is delivered and installed.

Liquidity Strengthened and Capital Structure Simplified

Innventure ended 2025 with $65.4 million in cash, restricted cash and equivalents, up from $11.1 million a year earlier thanks to financings and portfolio actions. In January 2026 the company added a $40 million registered direct offering and repaid $5.6 million of convertible notes, moves that bolster liquidity while simplifying the balance sheet for future growth.

Deep G&A Cuts Support a Leaner Corporate Platform

The company executed substantial cost reductions, cutting consolidated G&A from $29.7 million in the fourth quarter of 2024 to $11.5 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, a 61 percent drop. Parent‑level cash G&A also fell sharply to $5.7 million, with professional services trimmed by roughly 42 percent, and management signaled an ongoing commitment to a lean top‑company structure.

AeroFlexx: Commercial Traction and Aveda Partnership

AeroFlexx has now recorded six straight quarters of revenue across multiple end‑markets, demonstrating steady commercial traction for its packaging technology. The business also announced a global partnership with Aveda, part of the Estée Lauder portfolio, with select product launches slated for 2027 and a near‑term revenue pipeline of about $30 million, roughly one‑third already in final negotiations.

Refinity: Fast Technical Progress and Huge Market Opportunity

Refinity, formed in late 2024, produced its first metric ton of circular product from mixed plastic waste within a year, posting 60–70 percent yields versus around 25 percent for competing technologies. Management sees a path to a 10 kiloton‑per‑year demonstration plant by 2028 and a roughly 150 kiloton commercial facility early next decade, targeting a combined petrochemical and sustainable aviation fuel market that could reach tens of billions of dollars.

Capital Deployment Track Record and Net Asset Value Creation

Since inception, Innventure has deployed roughly $160 million of balance‑sheet capital into its operating companies and now estimates about $860 million of net asset value created. That figure includes approximately $460 million previously distributed to shareholders through PureCycle, which management cited as proof of its ability to convert invested capital into realizable value.

Persistent Adjusted EBITDA Loss and Profitability Timeline

Despite operational progress, profitability remains distant, with 2025 adjusted EBITDA showing a loss of $78.8 million after excluding a large non‑cash goodwill charge and other items. Management reiterated that consolidated cash‑flow breakeven is not expected until 2028, underscoring several more years of investment and scaling before the model turns sustainably cash‑generative.

Supply Chain Bottlenecks Create Revenue Recognition Risk

Executives warned that global data‑center construction bottlenecks and shortages of distribution equipment, switchgear, memory and mechanical systems may delay deliveries tied to Accelsius and other projects. As a result, 2026 revenue is expected to be heavily weighted to the back half of the year, making quarterly revenue trends lumpy and difficult to predict.

COGS Volatility, Inventory Write‑downs and Margin Uncertainty

Cost of goods sold has risen relative to revenue as operating companies build inventory for expected orders, shift to higher‑capacity products and absorb intangible amortization. Inventory declined by about $5 million quarter‑over‑quarter while revenue was under $2 million, reflecting obsolescence and write‑downs that create near‑term margin volatility and limit visibility into normalized unit economics.

Goodwill Overhang from Prior Non‑Cash Write‑downs

The company still carries roughly $23 million of goodwill on its balance sheet after a previous non‑cash adjustment of $347 million that heavily distorted reported net results. Management acknowledged this accounting history as a factor investors must consider when comparing past and future earnings trends and emphasized focusing on cash metrics and asset values.

Revenue Base Still Small Versus Ambitious Run‑Rate Goals

Even with rising bookings, Innventure’s 2025 revenue base of $2.1 million remains tiny compared with the projected run‑rate targets for businesses like Accelsius. The call made clear that the bulk of anticipated growth is expected in late 2026 and beyond, contingent on timely execution, installation and ramp‑up of customer deployments.

Dilution Trade‑offs as Operating Companies Raise Capital

To ease funding pressure on the parent, management is actively pushing operating companies to raise more capital independently at the subsidiary level. While this approach preserves Innventure’s cash, it will dilute its ownership stakes in the most successful assets over time, posing a strategic trade‑off between balance‑sheet strength and upside participation for shareholders.

AeroFlexx Minority Stake Limits Consolidated Upside

Innventure’s exposure to AeroFlexx is constrained by a minority ownership position, represented by about $19.5 million of equity and $9.2 million of debt securities on the balance sheet. This structure means that even if AeroFlexx scales meaningfully, the full value creation may not show up in Innventure’s consolidated revenue and earnings in a proportional way.

Limited Operational Detail Leaves Investors Wanting More

Management opted not to provide detailed disclosures on metrics such as megawatts per contract, pricing, or more granular pipeline economics, instead deferring them to future CEO‑level deep‑dive calls. While understandable given competitive sensitivities, this limited transparency reduces near‑term visibility for investors trying to model revenue trajectories and margins.

Forward Guidance: Toward Self‑Funding by 2028

Looking ahead, Innventure framed its platform as moving from a capital‑consuming model toward increasingly self‑funding operating companies over the next several years. The company is targeting consolidated cash‑flow positivity in 2028, with Accelsius expected to hit cash‑flow breakeven by the end of 2026 on the back of over $50 million of contracted backlog, while AeroFlexx and Refinity follow longer development arcs tied to sizable end‑markets.

Innventure’s earnings call painted a picture of a high‑potential but still early‑stage platform, with strong validation at Accelsius, promising pipelines at AeroFlexx and Refinity, and a much stronger balance sheet. Yet with modest current revenue, significant losses, supply‑chain timing risks and potential dilution, investors will need patience and risk tolerance as the company works to convert its robust bookings and technology bets into sustainable cash generation.

Tomi Environmental Solutions’ latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone as management balanced clear operational progress with stark financial constraints. Executives pointed to growing recurring revenue, margin expansion, and regulatory wins, yet acknowledged a steep revenue drop in 2025, thin cash reserves, and ongoing losses that keep liquidity and execution risk front and center.

Commercial and Integration Milestones

Tomi reported its first commercial SIS installation in June 2025 and ended the year with four fully operational SIS enclosures, marking tangible traction for its integration strategy. A $500,000 December contract from a global biopharma player for pass-through fill boxes and a combined $36 million opportunity book for integration and SteraMist iHP projects underscore the potential scale if the pipeline converts.

Strategic OEM and Distribution Partnerships

Partnerships with OEMs such as PBSC, ESCO, and Steelco are embedding SteraMist iHP into clean room enclosures and biosafety cabinets, with one PBSC pass-through project outperforming cycle-time expectations and a follow-on project already underway. International distributors, including ARES Scientific and U.K.-based TCA, are helping secure wins at universities and regional customers, broadening the company’s geographic and sector reach.

Recurring Revenue Traction and Backlog Growth

Management emphasized progress toward a more recurring model, noting that TOMI Support Services backlog grew 16% and BIT Solution backlog rose 24% in Q1 2026 versus a year earlier. Growing open BIT Solution orders and the shift to recurring open-order arrangements are central to the strategy of smoothing revenue volatility and building steadier cash flows.

Q1 2026 Revenue Momentum vs Prior Year

While full-year 2025 revenue declined, early 2026 trends are more encouraging, with management stating that Q1 2026 revenue exceeded Q1 2025. Solution sales climbed about 43.7% to $429,413, support services surged roughly 245.8% to $253,390, and iHP service invoices plus open orders rose about 66.1% to $729,440, signaling healthier demand across the portfolio.

Margin Improvement and Cost Discipline

Despite top-line pressure, Tomi expanded gross margin to approximately 55% in 2025 from 46% in 2024, a nine-point gain that reflects better mix and operational efficiency. Total operating expenses fell about 10% year over year, showing management’s focus on tightening costs even as it invests selectively in growth initiatives.

Improved Losses and Cash Flow Usage

Net loss narrowed to $3.7 million, or $0.19 per share, in 2025 from $4.5 million, or $0.22 per share, in 2024, an improvement of roughly 17.8% as margins improved and expenses were controlled. Operating cash usage also improved, with cash used in operations declining to $1.2 million from $1.4 million, suggesting incremental progress toward more sustainable cash dynamics.

Regulatory and Market Approvals Supporting New Verticals

A late-2025 FDA approval of hydrogen peroxide as a direct food additive is opening new food safety avenues, including engagements with large multinationals in dairy and nutrition. Additional regulatory recognition in the U.K. and the Netherlands, together with ongoing work on key U.S. labels and approvals, is gradually widening the addressable market, though some approvals are still pending.

Notable Customer Wins and Use-Cases

Tomi highlighted several marquee wins, including adoption of SteraMist iHP by a cell and gene therapy manufacturer for a commercial-scale pharma facility and selection by NASA’s Johnson Space Center for a biosecurity operation. An eye‑health customer expanded to two active facilities with multiple machines per site and is trialing a third location, while the Algafeed collaboration extends the platform into agricultural and food-related applications.

Intellectual Property, Reach and Industry Recognition

The company underscored its technology moat, noting more than 30 utility and design patents extending to 2038 and deployments in over 40 countries. Recognition as Disinfection Decontamination Products Company of the Year and compliance across multiple vendor management platforms reinforce its credibility with institutional buyers in regulated environments.

Financing Tools Put in Place

To shore up liquidity, Tomi raised $535,000 through a convertible note in 2025 and arranged a $20 million equity line, drawing about $94,000 so far, while also putting in place a sizable shelf registration. Management has also engaged a financial advisor to explore additional capital options, signaling an active approach to funding future growth and working capital needs.

Decline in Annual Revenue

Against these operational wins, full-year revenue fell sharply to $5.6 million in 2025 from $7.7 million in 2024, a drop of roughly 27.3%. Management attributed the decline largely to the timing of customer equipment purchases and deferred capital spending rather than lost deals, but the shortfall underscores the company’s sensitivity to CapEx cycles.

Weak Year-End Liquidity and Cash Position

The company closed 2025 with just about $88,000 in cash and around $1 million in working capital, levels that raise clear near-term liquidity concerns for investors. These constraints help explain the flurry of financing actions and heighten the importance of converting backlog and pipeline into cash-generating sales on a timely basis.

Continued Net Loss and Reliance on External Financing

Although losses narrowed, Tomi remains firmly unprofitable with a $3.7 million net loss in 2025, and management acknowledged ongoing reliance on external capital sources. The business model’s transition toward recurring revenue is still underway, meaning equity lines, notes, and other financings remain critical to support operations and growth projects.

Customer Deferrals and Macroeconomic Headwinds

Executives pointed to macro and political uncertainties, including tariffs and global conflicts, as drivers of customer caution and CapEx deferrals. One cited example involved a large university pushing major equipment spending to late 2026, illustrating how external factors can delay the realization of even well-qualified pipeline opportunities.

Partial Conversion of Pipeline to Recognized Revenue

From a previously announced $3 million integration pipeline in late 2025, only about $800,000 has been recognized so far, leaving several projects awaiting final approvals and execution. This gap between announced pipeline and realized revenue highlights timing and execution risk, which could weigh on results if delays persist or deals fail to close.

Scaling Constraints and Need for Capital

Management acknowledged that with a team of roughly 20 employees, the company would need more capital and headcount to scale production and service capacity for large new orders. While this lean structure helps control costs, it also limits how quickly Tomi can ramp if multiple sizable integration or OEM projects move forward at once.

Regulatory Work Still Required in Key Markets

Several important regulatory pathways remain unfinished, including certain environmental and product labels and industry-specific approvals that are vital for markets such as cannabis and biosafety cabinets. Until these are secured, revenue growth in these verticals will be constrained, making regulatory execution a critical factor in the growth story.

Stock Price Decline and Market Valuation Pressure

Management noted that the share price has fallen significantly from prior highs, with the stock trading well below a dollar at year-end and at the time of the call. This market pressure reflects investor concerns over near-term financial health and dilution risk, even as management argues that technology progress and partnerships are not yet fully appreciated.

Outlook and Forward Guidance

Looking to 2026, Tomi guided to roughly $12 million in revenue, about double 2025 levels, supported by a $16 million integration pipeline and $20 million in SteraMist iHP opportunities. Management expects around $3 million of sales from five named customers and roughly $6 million when including steady iHP deployment services, while noting that operating expenses should grow more slowly than revenue as backlog and recurring orders convert.

Tomi’s earnings call presented a story of promising technology and early commercial traction weighed down by tight liquidity and lumpy revenue. For investors, the key debate is whether the expanding pipeline, regulatory tailwinds, and recurring revenue gains can materialize fast enough to offset funding needs and macro-related delays, making execution over the next few quarters crucial for the stock’s trajectory.

Record Revenue Underscores Organic Growth Momentum

The performance signals that core demand across the portfolio is expanding despite price pressure in some markets and softer beverage trends.

Profitability Metrics Improve with Higher Adjusted EBITDA

Gross profit rose 6% year over year to $55 million, with margins edging down slightly to 27% from 28% as mix and input costs weighed on profitability. Adjusted EBITDA climbed 19% to $10.7 million, and executives reiterated their confidence in reaching $62 million to $72 million of adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2026.

International Cannabis Delivers Surging Volumes

International cannabis net sales jumped 73% from a year earlier to $24.1 million, rising 20% sequentially on what management called exceptional volume growth. Medical cannabis flower volumes doubled and oil volumes rose 90%, with Germany standing out at 43% year-over-year growth for the quarter.

Tilray Pharma Distribution Extends European Reach

Distribution revenue through Tilray Pharma hit $83 million, up 35% year over year and marking the strongest third quarter on record for the segment. With CC Pharma recognized as a top European innovator and new deals expanding German pharmacy coverage from 13,000 to 16,000 locations plus fresh UK access via Smartway, the company sees distribution as a key growth engine.

BrewDog Deal and Carlsberg Pact Build Beverage Scale

Tilray acquired BrewDog assets for about EUR 40 million, projecting that the transaction will add roughly $225 million to $250 million in revenue and help create a near $500 million global craft beverage platform on a pro forma basis. Management also flagged a U.S. licensing partnership beginning in 2027 with Carlsberg that will allow Tilray to produce, market, and distribute Carlsberg brands domestically, supporting a pro forma company run-rate around $1.2 billion.

Liquidity Improves with Net Cash Position

The company ended the quarter with $264.8 million in cash, restricted cash, and marketable securities, moving into a net cash position of about $3.5 million versus net debt a year ago. That shift represents an improvement of roughly $40.2 million and gives Tilray more flexibility to navigate volatility and integrate recent acquisitions.

Project 420 Delivers Targeted Cost Savings

Management reported the completion of Project 420 and the realization of its $33 million annualized cost-savings goal, aimed at resetting the beverage cost base and supporting margins. During the quarter alone, Tilray captured more than $6.2 million of annualized savings, which executives say should increasingly flow through to profitability as integration work continues.

Wellness Segment Grows with Better Margins

Wellness revenue climbed 16% year over year to $16.4 million, supported by new value-added products and targeted pricing initiatives. Segment gross margin edged up to 33% from 32%, suggesting that innovation and disciplined pricing are helping offset broader cost pressures.

Beverage Segment Faces Revenue and Margin Strain

Beverage net revenue declined to $42.6 million from $55.9 million in the prior-year quarter, reflecting distribution changes and category softness. Gross margin in the segment compressed to 32% from 36% due to lower production leverage and higher input costs, and management acknowledged that the beverage portfolio remains a work in progress despite early benefits from Project 420.

Commodity Cost Volatility Adds Margin Headwinds

Higher prices for aluminum, fuel, and hops continued to weigh on results, with aluminum alone adding almost $2.9 million in extra costs year to date. Tilray is hedging 65% to 75% of monthly aluminum purchases and extending coverage a year out, but executives cautioned that volatile input prices will remain a drag on margins.

Price Compression Erodes International Cannabis Revenue

While volumes surged in international cannabis, Tilray noted that price compression shaved roughly $7 million from revenue in the segment. The margin pressure partly offset the benefits of higher sales and underscores the competitive dynamics facing cannabis suppliers in key markets such as Europe.

Working Capital Build Weighs on Operating Cash Flow

Cash used in operations widened to $21.9 million from $5.8 million a year earlier, driven largely by inventory buildup ahead of a seasonally stronger fourth quarter and higher receivables tied to international growth. Excluding working capital, the business generated $3.4 million of operating cash, suggesting underlying operations are improving even as growth investments consume cash.

Regulatory Uncertainty Clouds U.S. Hemp and Cannabis

Management highlighted ongoing uncertainty around U.S. rescheduling efforts, potential pilot programs, and possible changes to hemp-derived THC rules that could curb products above certain thresholds. These moving pieces create risk for Tilray’s U.S. hemp-based beverage business and could reshape the opportunity set in that market over the next few years.

Integration Tasks Create Near-Term Operational Noise

The BrewDog acquisition, completed out of administration, requires substantial stabilization work, including catching up on supplier payments, rehiring and staffing, and restarting plant operations. Alongside SKU, plant, and distributor rationalizations and the timing of spring shelf resets, these efforts have caused temporary distribution losses and contributed to uneven beverage performance.

Net Loss Narrows Sharply but Profit Still Elusive

Tilray reported a net loss of $25.2 million for the quarter, a sharp improvement of $768.3 million from the prior year when results were weighed down by a large noncash impairment. Despite the progress, the company remains loss-making on a GAAP basis, leaving a clear profitability gap that management aims to close through growth, cost savings, and mix shifts.

Guidance Anchored by Cost Savings and Scale Gains

Looking ahead, Tilray reaffirmed its fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA target of $62 million to $72 million, pointing to record Q3 revenue, a 19% rise in adjusted EBITDA, and ongoing cost efficiencies as key supports. Management stressed that the completed $33 million cost-savings program, improving beverage margins near 32%, a diversified revenue mix across cannabis, distribution, beverage, and wellness, and active commodity hedging all underpin their confidence despite working capital drag and lingering market volatility.

Tilray’s earnings call painted the picture of a company gaining scale and operational discipline while still wrestling with margin pressure, integration complexities, and regulatory unknowns. For investors, the story hinges on whether rising international cannabis demand, an expanding beverage platform, and realized cost savings can ultimately translate into sustainable profitability and stronger cash generation.

This backlog is anchored by three large U.S. gas-fired plants totaling over 3.4 gigawatts, giving Argan multiyear visibility on revenues as these projects progress.

Power Segment Leadership

Diversified Segment Momentum

Stockholders’ equity stood at $462 million, and investment income alone contributed $7.7 million in the fourth quarter, reinforcing financial flexibility.

Capital returns were a clear priority, with $43 million returned to shareholders in fiscal 2026 through dividends and buybacks. The quarterly dividend was increased by 33% to $0.50 per share, marking a third straight annual raise, and the share repurchase authorization was lifted to $150 million, with about $114 million repurchased since late 2021.

Management highlighted the substantial completion of the Trumbull Energy Center in December 2025 ahead of schedule as a key operational win. Argan’s backlog remains heavily skewed toward natural gas, with roughly 77% tied to gas-fired projects, 14% to renewables and 9% to industrial work. The company noted ongoing constraints across craft labor, project leadership and back-office roles that limit how many large projects can be handled at once. Light electric vehicle (LEV) revenues in Q4 surged 524.2% to $12.92 million, underscoring rapid uptake in two-wheeler and related applications. Revenue from REV products for the full year climbed 252% to $36.36 million, signaling that newer platforms are gaining real commercial traction.

Battery Business Revenue Expansion

The core Battery segment continued to expand, with Q4 revenue rising 35.8% year over year to $30.82 million. For 2025, the Battery business contributed $105.98 million to consolidated revenue, confirming its role as the company’s main growth engine despite transitional challenges.

Cash Generation and Liquidity

CBAK ended 2025 with $75.68 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash, up from $60.79 million a year earlier, providing a buffer during the ramp period. Operating cash flow also improved, rising to $48.55 million from $39.70 million in 2024, indicating that growing sales are translating into real cash generation.

Capacity Additions and Product Launches

The company commissioned a new 40135 cell production line in Dalian at the end of 2025 with 2.3 GWh of capacity, aimed at high-demand applications. In Nanjing, Phase II added two new lines totaling 3.0 GWh atop the existing 1.5 GWh, with management noting that demand for 40135 cells already far exceeds available supply.

Strategic Vertical Integration and Asset Investment

Capital expenditures totaled $44.65 million in 2025, funding new facilities across Dalian, Nanjing, Zhejiang and Anhui as CBAK deepens its manufacturing footprint. Hitrans is building a 10,000 MT cathode plant and 37,000 MT precursor facility, scheduled to enter operation from 2026 through the first half of 2027, supporting a more vertically integrated cost base.

Commercial Partnerships and Market Expansion

Management spotlighted new and expanding partnerships with names such as SPIRO in Africa, Anker Innovation, Scania’s Posida unit, Ather Energy, Schneider ACE Battery and Inverted Energy. These ties, along with growth in India, Vietnam through DAT and African markets, position CBAK as an increasingly global supplier in LEV and storage niches.
